chunk_summary: This section outlines India's pathways for managing acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months, emphasizing identification, triage, and follow-up across community and facility-based care systems.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for diagnosing severe and moderate acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn the triage process for acute malnutrition, including danger signs and referral protocols.
Explore community-based and facility-based management pathways for acute malnutrition in India.
Recognize discharge criteria and relapse prevention strategies for malnourished children.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined by WHZ < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting oedema.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined by WHZ between -3 and -2 SD and/or MUAC 115–125 mm without oedema.
SAM with complications requires same-day referral to NRC or FB-SAM care; SAM without complications is managed in the community.
Discharge from facility-based care requires resolution of oedema, adequate intake on home-feasible foods, and follow-up accountability.
Infants under six months and children with major comorbidities require early escalation and breastfeeding restoration or safe feeding plans.
reference_quote: Treat acute malnutrition as a clinical emergency when any danger sign or complication is present: poor appetite/anorexia, fever or suspected sepsis, persistent vomiting, severe dehydration, respiratory distress, altered sensorium, hypoglycaemia suspicion, or oedema grade +++.
document_key_topics: Triage and referral protocols for malnourished children
